title: The Wink

jokes.text: When you wake up, it's like you're being born all over again. You can't see, you can't talk. You're on your way to the bathroom trying to remember, 'How do I walk?'
jokes.setup: Jerry describes the universal experience of waking up groggy
jokes.punchline: 'How do I walk?' — the exaggerated helplessness of morning stupor presented as genuine amnesia
jokes.explanation: Solid stand-up observation with good escalation — the specificity of 'How do I walk?' elevates it beyond generic morning-grumpiness material. Clean and relatable but not extraordinary.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know that moment when you first wake up and you don't know where you are? Your eyes are open but your brain is still asleep. You're like a newborn. You can't remember how to work your legs.

jokes.text: They should sell the snooze alarm with an unemployment application and a bottle of tequila. Just make it a complete, pathetic loser kit.
jokes.setup: The snooze alarm means you already know you're not getting up — you're already a failure
jokes.punchline: Bundle it as a 'complete, pathetic loser kit' with unemployment app and tequila
jokes.explanation: One of Jerry's strongest stand-up bits in this episode. The 'loser kit' phrasing is genuinely inventive — the three-item bundle (snooze alarm, unemployment app, tequila) builds perfectly and the 'pathetic loser kit' label is a memorable, quotable phrase. The logic is airtight and the image is vivid.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what the snooze button is? It's the worst invention of all time. It's a monument to human weakness. It's like we looked at the alarm clock and said, 'You know what this needs? A button that lets me lie to myself.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Because that's what snoozing is — it's negotiating with reality. 'Okay, alarm, you got me up, but I'm not ready to be awake yet. How about nine more minutes where I can pretend I'm still asleep and you pretend you didn't go off?'

jokes.text: A woman with a sexy voice like yours, it's hard to believe you're waking up alone.
jokes.setup: James is ostensibly a professional wake-up service caller doing his job
jokes.punchline: He pivots to flirting with a customer he's never met, based solely on her voice
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the absurdity of a professional crossing a line with a customer at 7:15am. Elaine's flustered response sells it. Functional setup for the B-plot but not a standout joke on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what they should make? A 'Complete Pathetic Loser Kit.' It comes with a snooze alarm clock, a t-shirt with a picture of a snooze alarm clock on it, a poster of a snooze alarm clock, a mug with a snooze alarm clock, a pair of pajamas with little snooze alarm clocks all over it, and a video called 'How to Use Your Snooze Alarm Clock.'

jokes.text: Tri-State-wake-up service... person. Call me James.
jokes.setup: Elaine reaches for a professional title for James and can't find one
jokes.punchline: '...person.' — the complete deflation of any professional dignity, followed by the overfamiliar 'Call me James'
jokes.explanation: The pause before '...person' is a small but well-executed beat. The shift to 'Call me James' immediately after deepens the absurdity of the situation. Economy is high — this is two jokes in five words.

dialogue.speaker: James
dialogue.line: Hi, I'd like to set up a wake-up call for tomorrow morning at 6 AM.

dialogue.speaker: Wake-up Service Operator
dialogue.line: Of course, sir. What's your room number?

dialogue.speaker: Wake-up Service Operator
dialogue.line: Room 412... Well, I'll make sure to give you a personal wake-up call. Maybe I'll stay on the line a little longer than usual.

dialogue.speaker: James
dialogue.line: That's... that's not necessary.

dialogue.speaker: Wake-up Service Operator
dialogue.line: Oh, I insist. I have a feeling you're going to be one of my favorite calls.

jokes.text: At least I've spoken to my guy. You're going out on a deaf date.
jokes.setup: Jerry teases Elaine about going on a blind date with her wake-up guy (whom she's never seen); she retorts that Jerry is going on a blind date with his cousin Holly
jokes.punchline: 'Deaf date' — a play on 'blind date' because she's only heard James, not seen him, while Jerry has only seen pictures of Holly
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely sharp wordplay joke. 'Deaf date' is an elegant inversion of 'blind date' that is logically consistent and hasn't been done to death. It's Elaine at her wittiest — a confident, fast comeback. High quotability, strong economy. Earns a high score.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, when someone calls you first thing in the morning, it's very intimate. I mean, you're in bed, you're half-asleep, you're vulnerable. You're probably not even wearing...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! We don't need to hear this!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, we get the idea.

jokes.text: Would you rather date the blind or the deaf? — I think I would rather date the deaf. Because the blind would be a little messy around the house. They're not gonna get all the crumbs. You're constantly walking around with a sponge.
jokes.setup: Jerry and George debate whether it's better to date a blind or deaf person
jokes.punchline: George's reason for preferring the deaf is purely selfish domestic logic — the blind leave crumbs
jokes.explanation: Classic Seinfeld 'about nothing' riff. George's crumb logic is pure character comedy — he's reducing a major human condition to a housekeeping inconvenience. The specificity of 'you're constantly walking around with a sponge' is excellent. Feels very George.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I have a blind date tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: A blind date? Well, at least you won't have to worry about her seeing you.

jokes.text: See, I disagree. I would rather date the blind. You could let the house go, you let yourself go. A good-looking blind woman doesn't really know you're not good enough for her.
jokes.setup: Jerry counters George's crumb argument for dating the deaf
jokes.punchline: Jerry's reason is even more self-serving: a blind woman can't tell she's dating below her league
jokes.explanation: The escalation from George's crumb logic to Jerry's self-deprecating vanity logic is perfectly structured. 'She'd figure it out soon enough' as the capper from George is beautifully timed. Dark but delivered with such casual confidence it lands as sharp observational comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: So what if you're blind and deaf? How do you know you're on a date?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, someone's got to be holding your arm and talking really loud.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious. What constitutes a date? Is it the intention? The location? The mutual understanding?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, it's a date if two people agree to spend time together romantically.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: But how would a blind, deaf person know that? They could be at a bus stop for all they know.

jokes.text: She'd figure it out soon enough.
jokes.setup: Jerry claims a blind woman wouldn't know she's dating below her league
jokes.punchline: George flatly implies she'd still figure out Jerry's inadequacy via other senses
jokes.explanation: Five words, perfectly placed. It's the button on the blind/deaf riff and the single best line in the exchange. Maximum economy — the joke is entirely in the implication. George's deadpan delivery would make this land huge. Genuinely excellent comic economy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I'd rather date a deaf woman than a blind woman. You know why? Because if she can't hear me, at least she can see that the apartment is a mess. But if she's blind, she won't know the apartment is a mess, but she'll hear me complaining about it all the time.

jokes.text: What are you turning into? — A healthy person.
jokes.setup: George reacts with bewilderment when Jerry produces a veggie sandwich and grapefruit instead of a normal breakfast
jokes.punchline: Jerry's earnest 'A healthy person' — delivered as if this is a reasonable and unremarkable answer
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the grapefruit plot mechanic. The joke itself is mild — the real comedy comes from what follows. Not particularly original but serves the narrative.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm for blind dates. I think blind dates are great. Because if a woman can't see you, there's still a chance she might like your personality.

jokes.text: Ow! Ow! You squirted me. Oh, sorry. Boy, that stings.
jokes.setup: Jerry is eating a grapefruit; George is seated across from him
jokes.punchline: Jerry accidentally squirts grapefruit juice into George's eye — the inciting incident for the entire winking subplot
jokes.explanation: The grapefruit squirt is simultaneously a physical gag and the engine of the episode's main plot. On first viewing it's a mild slapstick laugh; on rewatch you appreciate how elegantly it launches the entire winking misunderstanding. High rewatch bonus for plot architecture.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Jerry, let me tell you something. You're sitting there fantasizing about these perfect blind dates, but in reality, you're going to show up, she's going to take one look at you, and spend the whole evening texting her friends about how she's being held hostage by some guy with a ridiculous shirt.

jokes.text: What percentage of people are good-looking? — Twenty-five percent. — No way. It's like 4 to 6 percent. It's a 20-1 shot.
jokes.setup: Elaine is optimistic about her blind date being good-looking
jokes.punchline: George reduces attraction to brutal actuarial statistics — 4 to 6 percent, a '20-1 shot'
jokes.explanation: George applying gambling odds to human attractiveness is perfectly in character. The specificity of '20-1 shot' makes it funnier than a vague percentage. Pure George Costanza logic — pessimistic, cynical, and mathematically presented.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So I'm eating this grapefruit, right? Very healthy. I'm being very responsible.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's great, Jerry. Really great.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I cut it in half, I'm squeezing it...

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Uh, Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: My eye! You squirted grapefruit in my eye!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I'm sorry! Here, let me—

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Don't touch me! This is what I get for encouraging your health kick!

jokes.text: Have you been to the motor vehicle bureau? It's a leper colony down there.
jokes.setup: Jerry disputes George's claim that only 4-6% of people are good-looking
jokes.punchline: George cites the DMV as empirical evidence — 'a leper colony'
jokes.explanation: A sharp, quotable line. The DMV as a repository of the world's least attractive people is an observation that feels specific and true. 'Leper colony' is a perfectly calibrated hyperbole. Classic George delivering a cynical worldview with total conviction.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I can tell just by his voice that he's good-looking.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you, a bat? You're using echolocation now?

jokes.text: So basically what you're saying is, 95 percent of the population is undateable? — Undateable!
jokes.setup: George has been arguing that very few people are attractive
jokes.punchline: Elaine summarizes to the logical extreme; George enthusiastically confirms with 'Undateable!' — a near-neologism delivered with relish
jokes.explanation: George's emphatic 'Undateable!' is the payoff to the whole attractiveness riff. It's a great word-as-punchline moment — the audience gets George's delight in his own pessimistic conclusion. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So you're telling me that 80% of women find 20% of men attractive? That means I'm in the 80% that nobody wants.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's not how statistics work.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because the math checks out perfectly in my head, and it's very depressing.

jokes.text: Then how are all these people getting together? — Alcohol.
jokes.setup: Elaine points out the contradiction: if 95% are undateable, how does the population reproduce?
jokes.punchline: Jerry's one-word answer: 'Alcohol.'
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The single-word answer is the pinnacle of comedic economy — the setup is a genuine logical paradox, and 'Alcohol.' resolves it with total confidence and zero elaboration. It's quotable, cynically true, and perfectly timed. The structure is flawless: big question, tiny answer.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You want to know what it's like? Go to the DMV. That's what it's like. It's like a biblical disease colony there. I'm telling you, if they ever need a leper colony, they don't have to build anything new, they can just use the DMV waiting area.

jokes.text: You keep winking at me. That's really obnoxious. — I had no idea. — Right there! You just did it again.
jokes.setup: George's eye has been irritated by grapefruit juice; he is involuntarily winking
jokes.punchline: Wilhelm calls him out for winking — George has no idea he's doing it, but Wilhelm catches him doing it again immediately
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the grapefruit gag. The escalating 'Right there! You just did it again' is a well-executed running visual gag. The comedy of George being caught winking while denying he winked is solid cringe comedy — he's trapped.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You've been dating for fifteen years, and in that time you've had sixty-three girlfriends. That means the average length of your relationships is about three months.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, when you put it like that, it sounds bad.

jokes.text: Pulp can move, baby!
jokes.setup: Jerry argues that grapefruit pulp couldn't travel across a table to get in George's eye
jokes.punchline: George's indignant, passionate defense of pulp's mobility — 'Pulp can move, baby!'
jokes.explanation: Pure George. The outrage, the specificity, the 'baby' — this is George Costanza at maximum indignation over something trivial. Extremely quotable and perfectly character-integrated. 'Pulp can move, baby!' is the kind of line that gets repeated by Seinfeld fans.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How do ugly people find love?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Alcohol.

jokes.text: If I have to take out an eye, that's the breaks.
jokes.setup: George accuses Jerry's healthy eating of causing his eye injury
jokes.punchline: Jerry shrugs off the possibility of losing an eye as an acceptable cost of healthy eating
jokes.explanation: Jerry's casual acceptance of permanent eye damage in the service of healthy eating is a nice dark-comic inversion. The breezy 'that's the breaks' is perfectly dismissive. Good economy and character voice.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Pulp can't travel across the table like that.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What are you talking about? Of course it can travel across the table. Pulp travels all the time.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Not that far.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: George, pulp is very mobile. It's one of the most mobile food substances there is. It's practically commuting.

jokes.text: That's why Mr. Wilhelm was acting so misterioso.
jokes.setup: George realizes he's been involuntarily winking at his boss all morning
jokes.punchline: 'Misterioso' — George's invented Spanish-inflected adjective, delivered with total self-seriousness
jokes.explanation: George's vocabulary is always specific and slightly wrong. 'Misterioso' is a perfect George word — pseudo-sophisticated, slightly ridiculous, deployed with complete confidence. Character integration is high.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I had an eye injury and everyone at work thought I was saying things wrong. I kept saying 'misterioso' instead of 'mysterious' and they all just went along with it.

jokes.text: He thought I was hiding something about Morgan.
jokes.setup: Wilhelm saw George winking and interpreted it as a knowing signal about his subordinate Morgan
jokes.punchline: The irony: George's involuntary eye twitch was mistaken for conspiratorial insider knowledge
jokes.explanation: The situational irony of the wink subplot paying off is well-constructed. Not a laugh-out-loud joke but a satisfying plot mechanics moment with comedic resonance.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey there, Reggie!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Reggie?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, Reggie Jackson!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You're Betty! Betty Cooper!

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: And what does that make me?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Archie! Archie Andrews!

jokes.text: Hey, guys. — Hey, Jughead. — Hello, Archie. — Veronica. — Mr. Weatherbee.
jokes.setup: Kramer arrives at Jerry's apartment
jokes.punchline: The gang greets each other using Archie Comics character names in a seemingly pre-established ritual — Kramer is Jughead, Jerry is Archie, Elaine is Veronica, and Kramer calls George 'Mr. Weatherbee' (the principal, not a teen)
jokes.explanation: This is a genuinely delightful bit. The Archie Comics mapping is funny on its own but the joke is that George gets assigned the pompous adult authority figure (Mr. Weatherbee) rather than a teen character — a perfect encapsulation of how George is perceived. The 'Whoa.' reaction to George's designation is the button. High craft: unexpected, character-specific, economical.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: You could sell all this Yankees memorabilia. You'd make a fortune.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Oh sure, Newman. And then what? I'll have nothing to live for.

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Exactly.

jokes.text: Kramer's stunned 'Whoa.' reaction to being called Mr. Weatherbee — clearly a visible double-take at George
jokes.setup: Kramer assigns each person an Archie character name and reaches George
jokes.punchline: Kramer's own surprised 'Whoa' at what came out of his mouth — even he's startled by calling George Mr. Weatherbee
jokes.explanation: The 'Whoa.' is the button on the naming bit — Kramer surprising himself is very on-brand. A great reaction beat that elevates the previous joke.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: So Jerry, what exactly do you eat?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I eat... things that don't have a face.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Things that don't have a—

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Vegetables, fruits, grains...

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: So you're a...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I prefer not to use labels.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: It's just a word, Jerry.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's not *just* a word. It's a lifestyle choice that people judge you for.

jokes.text: Is this Don Mattingly's signature? — Yeah. — And Buck Showalter's? — It's an interoffice envelope. It gets passed around all over.
jokes.setup: Kramer spots what appears to be a valuable Yankees autograph
jokes.punchline: George deflates it — it's just a mundane interoffice envelope signed by anyone it passed through
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the autograph subplot. The comedy is in the gap between Kramer's excitement and the mundane reality. Sets up the escalating absurdity of the birthday card scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: You want the salad?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Yes, the salad.

dialogue.speaker: Waiter
dialogue.line: The salad. You're sure? Not the filet mignon? Not the ribeye? The salad.

jokes.text: Like I'm gonna risk my job with the New York Yankees to make a few extra bucks.
jokes.setup: Kramer suggests exploiting Yankees autographs for profit at George's workplace
jokes.punchline: George's righteous refusal — which the audience knows he will immediately abandon
jokes.explanation: The irony is rich because we know George will do exactly this. His self-righteous refusals before doing the bad thing are a classic George beat. On rewatch it's funnier because you know what's coming.

jokes.text: An envelope doesn't really cut it. — What is this? — A birthday card. — Signed by the entire Yankee organization?
jokes.setup: Kramer dismisses the signed envelope as not valuable enough
jokes.punchline: George produces Mr. Steinbrenner's birthday card, signed by the entire Yankees organization — an immediate and ludicrous upgrade
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'signed envelope' to 'entire Yankee organization's birthday card' is a nice comedic beat. Kramer's 'This could be worth something' is well-timed. The plot engine fires up nicely.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Does ordering a salad emasculate me?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Of course it does. You might as well be wearing a dress and holding a pocketbook.

jokes.text: Hello? / Elaine? / James! / Oh. Hello. Whew!
jokes.setup: Elaine answers the door expecting someone else; it's James, the wake-up guy she's only spoken to on the phone.
jokes.punchline: Elaine's 'Whew!' reaction beat — presumably he is very attractive, which subverts the 4-6% odds the group just discussed.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the 'deaf date' and odds discussion — the audience understands the 'Whew!' means James defied the 20-1 odds. A visual payoff to a conceptual setup.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't understand why ordering a salad makes you less of a man. It's a salad. You're eating vegetables.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah! Exactly! I mean, what's the difference? A salad is food. Food is food.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Right. But somehow in the male psyche, a salad is considered feminine.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's ridiculous. I eat salads all the time.

jokes.text: You don't eat meat? What are you, one of those...? — No, I'm not... one of those.
jokes.setup: Jerry is taken to a steakhouse; Holly discovers he doesn't eat meat
jokes.punchline: Holly can't bring herself to say 'vegetarian' and trails off with '...one of those'; Jerry hastily denies being '...one of those' — both parties conspire to avoid the word
jokes.explanation: The shared horror at the word 'vegetarian' — unspoken between both characters — is a sharp observational comedy bit about social stigma around dietary choices. The mutual avoidance of naming it is funny and economical.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So what did you have for dinner at Holly's?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: It was meat.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Meat? What kind of meat?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say it came from an animal that had a lot of legs.

jokes.text: Grandma Mema? — Elaine must have mentioned Grandma Mema. — No, I think I would have remembered Mema.
jokes.setup: Holly references a family figure as if Jerry should know her
jokes.punchline: Jerry's dry 'I think I would have remembered Mema' — implying the name is so ridiculous it's unforgettable
jokes.explanation: The recurring 'Mema' joke works because the name is inherently funny and Holly treats it with complete earnestness. Jerry's reaction is perfectly calibrated. Sets up the running Mema thread.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm packing heat... straight to my arteries.

jokes.text: That's typical. Elaine never liked Grandma Mema.
jokes.setup: Jerry says he doesn't know who Mema is
jokes.punchline: Holly immediately interprets this as Elaine's deliberate omission due to family resentment — a fully formed family drama in one sentence
jokes.explanation: Holly's instant pivot to family grudge is funny and economical — we get an entire backstory of Elaine-Mema tension in one dismissive sentence. Good character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I wasn't winking! I have something in my eye!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: Sure you do.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, I'm serious! I got hit in the eye!

dialogue.speaker: Newman
dialogue.line: That's a likely story.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Why would I wink at you? What are we, in some kind of secret society?

jokes.text: Jerry orders 'just a salad' at a steakhouse — Holly and the waiter's visible reaction
jokes.setup: Holly has ordered a porterhouse; Jerry struggles through the entire menu trying to find something light
jokes.punchline: Jerry settles on 'just a salad' — the most emasculating possible order at a famous steakhouse
jokes.explanation: The comedy builds through Jerry's increasingly futile attempt to find something non-meaty at a steakhouse — the 'full bird stuffed with ham topped with Gorgonzola' is a great escalating absurdity before the deflation of 'just a salad.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm eating this mutton. I'm eating it because I'm a man, and men eat meat. Red meat. Rare meat. The kind of meat that makes other animals nervous.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: That's not healthy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I don't care. Real men don't worry about their arteries. We worry about important things. Like sports. And other men.

jokes.text: The chicken is a full bird, stuffed with ham, topped with Gorgonzola.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks for something lighter than steak
jokes.punchline: The waiter describes the 'lighter' chicken option as a full bird stuffed with ham and covered in cheese — every item makes it worse
jokes.explanation: An excellent absurdist restaurant joke. Each element (full bird, stuffed with ham, topped with Gorgonzola) escalates the caloric horror while being presented as a matter-of-fact menu item. The structure is perfect — three escalating elements.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Hey, I need that card back.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: No! I like it!

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Look, I'll make you a deal. I know a guy, a baseball player. I can get him to hit home runs on command. Any time you want — you just say the word.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Yeah, yeah. You want a home run? Boom! Home run. Every time.

jokes.text: Thank you. — [beat] — Just a salad.
jokes.setup: Jerry has just ordered 'just a salad' at a famous steakhouse
jokes.punchline: Holly repeats 'Just a salad.' to the waiter — or to herself — as a damning verdict
jokes.explanation: The echo of 'just a salad' as a reaction beat is perfectly placed. Holly's disbelief/disappointment is conveyed entirely through repetition. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about you. About your whole situation. And I just want to say, you're an inspiration to me.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, you got any baseball cards?

jokes.text: When you live alone, your dogs are all you have. — Do you like dogs? — Shut... up! You stupid little mutt!
jokes.setup: James poignantly says his dogs are all he has; Elaine tries to connect by asking if he likes dogs
jokes.punchline: James immediately screams at his dogs, destroying the tender moment
jokes.explanation: A beautifully structured subversion — the sentimental build ('all you have') punctured instantly by James screaming at the very dogs he claimed were his only companions. The speed of the reversal is key to why it works.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: This mutton is absolutely delicious! I'm in mutton heaven!

jokes.text: Dogs? Oh, I... I love dogs. — [dogs immediately growl at Elaine]
jokes.setup: Elaine desperately covers for her obvious discomfort with James's dogs by claiming to love them
jokes.punchline: The dogs immediately growl at her — animals detecting her lie
jokes.explanation: Elaine's lie being immediately exposed by animal instinct is a satisfying comedic beat. The dogs as lie detectors is a fun visual gag. Sets up the dog-chasing subplot.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, I like to think of myself as a man who can chew the fat... and literally chew the fat.

jokes.text: Maybe his dogs heard about how you tried to kidnap that other dog. These mutts like to gossip.
jokes.setup: Elaine can't understand why James's dogs hated her immediately
jokes.punchline: Jerry suggests dogs gossip about Elaine's past canine crimes via a dog network
jokes.explanation: 'These mutts like to gossip' is a great absurdist line — the image of dogs maintaining a gossip network about human behavior is inherently funny. Good callback to Elaine's history with dogs. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm out of here. And don't expect me back until you people learn to act like a normal family. This is insane!

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Elaine, wait—

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No, Jerry. I'm done. You all make me want to pull my hair out. Have fun with your mutton!

jokes.text: She thought it was kind of strange to just order a salad. You know... for a man.
jokes.setup: George asks how his lunch with Holly went
jokes.punchline: Holly's verdict: ordering a salad is 'strange' — specifically 'for a man'
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the salad joke — Holly's gendered verdict on Jerry's ordering. The pause before 'for a man' is a good comedic beat. Functional callback.

dialogue.speaker: James
dialogue.line: You could stay at my place.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh, that's very kind of you.

dialogue.speaker: James
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the thing is... I only have one bed.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh.

dialogue.speaker: James
dialogue.line: But don't worry, I'm very small.

jokes.text: Like a quiche thing? — You're in the ballpark.
jokes.setup: George is told Holly found the salad order strange 'for a man'
jokes.punchline: George's 'quiche thing' shorthand for unmasculine food choices; Jerry's 'you're in the ballpark' confirmation
jokes.explanation: 'Quiche thing' as a cultural shorthand for feminized food is a good George bit. Jerry's 'you're in the ballpark' is a nice deadpan confirmation. Both lines are economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me you've been walking around with raw meat in your jacket pockets?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Not raw meat, it was cooked mutton. And it was in napkins.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Napkins?! Jerry, that's disgusting! Do you know what that means?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's why dogs have been chasing me! I've been near you!

jokes.text: Salad. What was I thinking? Women don't respect salad-eaters.
jokes.setup: Jerry processes George's feedback about Holly's reaction
jokes.punchline: Jerry's self-recrimination lands on 'women don't respect salad-eaters' as an accepted axiom
jokes.explanation: The Seinfeld world's gendered food rules, delivered as received wisdom. Competent but not remarkable.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: So I'm sleeping on the couch, and he's in the bed.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Well, that's the way it should be. I mean, you got the back of the couch for support.

jokes.text: I don't know, but I'm sure it had parents.
jokes.setup: George asks what Holly is making for dinner, noting it's probably meat
jokes.punchline: Jerry's 'I'm sure it had parents' — a darkly witty reference to animal-based food delivered with total nonchalance
jokes.explanation: Excellent line. 'I'm sure it had parents' is a quietly brilliant way to reference meat-eating — it evokes the animal without saying it, with a darkly comic tenderness. High quotability, great economy. This is Jerry at his best.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I promised him I'd hit a home run.

dialogue.speaker: Paul O'Neill
dialogue.line: You can't just promise a kid you're gonna hit a home run. I've been playing professional baseball my whole life, and I can't guarantee I'm gonna hit a home run in any given at-bat.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, I promised him.

dialogue.speaker: Paul O'Neill
dialogue.line: That's not how baseball works.

jokes.text: Oh, don't worry. I'll be there. And I'll be packing an artery.
jokes.setup: George decides to come to Holly's dinner to demonstrate he eats real food
jokes.punchline: 'I'll be packing an artery' — a play on 'packing heat' (bringing a gun) applied to artery-clogging food
jokes.explanation: A sharp wordplay joke — 'packing an artery' as a riff on 'packing heat' is inventive and perfectly George. It's a brag about his meat-eating machismo delivered as if it's a tactical decision. High quotability and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: You know, Babe Ruth promised he'd hit a home run, and he did it. He pointed to the bleachers and he did it.

dialogue.speaker: O'Neill
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, Babe Ruth also struck out. A lot. And he couldn't hit a curveball to save his life in his later years.

jokes.text: What is this? — Your cut of the loot. Stubs gave me $200 for the autographed birthday card inside. — Who told you to sell the card? — You did. — No, I didn't. — Not in so many words, but I believe we had an understanding.
jokes.setup: George gave Kramer the birthday card envelope; Kramer sold it to a memorabilia dealer
jokes.punchline: Kramer claims George 'winked' his consent — George had no idea; the wink was a physical tic
jokes.explanation: The wink plot fully converges here in a beautifully constructed comedic misunderstanding. 'Not in so many words, but I believe we had an understanding' is a perfectly Kramerian line — he read a wink as a business agreement. The plot mechanics are elegant and this scene is the centerpiece of the episode's comic architecture.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: He promised me two home runs! Where's my second one?

jokes.text: I was not winking, you idiot! That was the grapefruit! It's like acid!
jokes.setup: Kramer insists George winked his consent to sell the card
jokes.punchline: George's outraged explanation — the wink was grapefruit juice damage — delivered at maximum volume
jokes.explanation: George's rage is perfectly calibrated. 'It's like acid!' is a great escalation of his earlier 'Boy, that stings.' His inability to control outcomes he set in motion is pure George Costanza tragedy-comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: The butcher was being so aloof with me.

dialogue.speaker: Holly
dialogue.line: Oh, that's just how butchers are. They're all like that.

jokes.text: Stubs already sold it to some guy whose kid's in the hospital.
jokes.setup: George demands they get the card back
jokes.punchline: It's been sold to a father whose child is in the hospital — instantly making George the villain if he reclaims it
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation. Just as George thinks he can fix his problem, the stakes are raised to morally impossible heights — reclaiming a card from a sick child. Dark but the show earns it through Seinfeld's established amorality. Sets up Kramer's hospital negotiation.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: It's a triple! It's a triple with an error!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: No, it's not. A triple is when you hit the ball and reach third base safely. If there's an error, it's not a triple, it's a hit and an error.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Well, what do you call it then?

jokes.text: Special occasions? — It wasn't special when my family visited?
jokes.setup: Holly explains Grandma Mema only used certain napkins on special occasions
jokes.punchline: Elaine immediately takes offense — revealing the depth of the family dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Elaine's instant wounded response reveals years of family resentment. The speed of her pivot from polite to aggrieved is well-executed cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, it's funny you should mention that. I was at the doctor's office the other day, and he's telling me about this heart surgery procedure. And I'm thinking, 'Doc, I appreciate the concern, but I'm not ready to have someone cracking open my chest like a pork chop at a barbecue.'

jokes.text: Everybody like mutton? Mm. Mutton! Hope you didn't cut the fat off.
jokes.setup: George has promised to 'pack an artery' and prove he's a real meat-eater
jokes.punchline: George's theatrical enthusiasm for mutton — an archaic meat — delivered with carnivore gusto
jokes.explanation: George performing extreme meat enthusiasm is a good character beat. The choice of 'mutton' specifically is funny — it's an almost cartoonishly manly meat. 'Hope you didn't cut the fat off' is the right level of performative machismo.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You know, these are the kinds of things that destroy civilizations. Not wars, not famine — napkins. It's always the napkins.

jokes.text: Kramer at the hospital negotiating with a sick boy for the birthday card
jokes.setup: Kramer needs to retrieve the Steinbrenner birthday card from a sick child
jokes.punchline: The entire premise: Kramer trying to negotiate a trade with a hospitalized child is inherently dark and absurd
jokes.explanation: The setup of Kramer hustling a sick child for a birthday card is the episode's darkest and most ambitious comedic premise. It works because Kramer is completely earnest — he's not being cruel, just obliviously self-interested. The scene's darkness is earned by the show's consistent moral universe.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, I can do it. I can hit a home run off Nolan Ryan.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Kramer, Nolan Ryan throws 98 miles per hour.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: So? I've got quick reflexes.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: You can't even catch a ball that's thrown to you.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: That's different. This is baseball.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Kramer, you haven't played baseball since high school.

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Exactly. I'm due.

jokes.text: What if I get Paul O'Neill to hit a home run tomorrow just for you?
jokes.setup: Bobby refuses to give up the card for anything
jokes.punchline: Kramer offers to deliver a made-to-order home run from a professional baseball player — a promise he has absolutely no ability to keep
jokes.explanation: This is Kramer at his most Kramer — confidently promising the impossible with total sincerity. The audacity of telling a sick child you can order a major leaguer to hit a home run 'just for you' is both hilarious and darkly sweet. High character integration: only Kramer could deliver this with a straight face.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: So let me get this straight. You're telling me that my grandmother's linen napkins - the ones she brought from the old country - are now dog bandanas?

dialogue.speaker: Kramer
dialogue.line: Not bandanas, Jerry. Collar accessories. Very fashionable.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: How did this even happen?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I may have put some pastrami in my pocket...

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: You put meat in your pocket?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I was saving it for later! Your dog smelled it, went crazy, and just... destroyed everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: My dog destroyed priceless heirlooms because you can't use a plate like a normal person?

jokes.text: Could he hit two home runs?
jokes.setup: Kramer offers one home run as a trade
jokes.punchline: Bobby, with a sick child's ruthless negotiating instinct, asks for two
jokes.explanation: Bobby's counter-negotiation is perfect. The sick child immediately trying to up the ask is darkly funny and completely logical — why settle for one home run when you can get two? The economy is flawless: four words, huge laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: Costanza, this mounted birthday card for Joe Morgan is brilliant. You really went the extra mile.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Thank you, sir. I thought it showed real class.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: You know what this tells me? Morgan respects you. He respects the organization. That's why he didn't show up to the party.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: He's making a statement.

jokes.text: Sure, kid, but then you got to promise you'll do something for me. — I know. — Get out of this bed one day and walk again. — That would be nice, but I really just need the card.
jokes.setup: Kramer agrees to two home runs but says Bobby has to do something in return
jokes.punchline: Kramer sets up the expectation that he'll ask for something moving/inspirational (walking again), but immediately deflates it: 'I really just need the card.'
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode. The structure is flawless: Kramer accidentally invokes the heartwarming hospitalized-child trope ('get out of this bed and walk again'), which sounds like it could be a genuine redemptive moment — then immediately pivots back to his actual selfish goal ('I really just need the card'). The misdirection is perfect, the dark subversion is earned, and Bobby's flat delivery of the punchline is the ideal button. High marks across all criteria.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, you know, Mr. Steinbrenner, I appreciate that. But honestly, the last thing I need is to be noticed. I've spent my entire life trying not to be noticed. Do you know how hard I've worked to fade into the background? And now you're telling me I'm finally going to get recognition? This is a nightmare.

jokes.text: Hey, salad's got nuttin' on this mutton.
jokes.setup: Holly is pleased Jerry is enjoying her mutton at dinner
jokes.punchline: 'Salad's got nuttin' on this mutton' — an internal rhyme delivered as a compliment
jokes.explanation: A tight rhyming pun that lands as both a callback to the salad embarrassment and a genuine wordplay joke. 'Nuttin'/'mutton' is the kind of New York-inflected wordplay that feels very Seinfeld. The show rewards this with the butcher callback, indicating it's a well-planted piece of wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Steinbrenner
dialogue.line: I've fired a lot of people. Let me see... there's Billy Martin, Billy Martin again, Billy Martin a third time, Billy Martin for the fourth time, and Billy Martin once more.

jokes.text: I wish I could take credit for it. It's a line my butcher uses when we're chewing the fat.
jokes.setup: Holly is charmed by Jerry's mutton/nutton line
jokes.punchline: 'Chewing the fat' is both the idiom for chatting and a literal description of what you do with your butcher, delivered completely straight
jokes.explanation: 'Chewing the fat' as a double entendre in a butcher context is a well-executed wordplay beat — subtle enough that it works as a straight line even if you miss the pun. Clean and smart.

jokes.text: What'd you do, ransack the place after she died?
jokes.setup: Holly keeps pointing out increasingly expensive Grandma Mema items that now decorate her apartment
jokes.punchline: Elaine's blunt accusation that Holly looted the estate immediately after Mema's death
jokes.explanation: Elaine's frustrated bluntness perfectly escalates the Mema running gag. 'Ransack the place after she died' is the kind of accusation only someone who has been hearing about Mema's possessions for too long would make. Good economy and character voice.

jokes.text: God forbid I should borrow one from Holly. It might have belonged to Grandma Mema.
jokes.setup: Elaine is complaining about having to borrow Jerry's jacket
jokes.punchline: Her fear that anything Holly owns might be a Mema artifact — confirming the running gag has reached full comic saturation
jokes.explanation: Good running gag callback — Elaine has been so primed by Holly's Mema possessiveness that she now fears any item in Holly's apartment. The logic is sound and the delivery is dry.

jokes.text: Thanks for mutton.
jokes.setup: Elaine is leaving Holly's dinner party and thanks her
jokes.punchline: 'Thanks for mutton' — a pun on 'thanks for nothing,' delivered as a genuine goodbye
jokes.explanation: A perfect three-word exit line. 'Thanks for mutton' lands both as a thank-you pun and a passive-aggressive insult ('thanks for nothing'). Maximum economy — it's the ideal button for the entire dinner party sequence. High quotability.

jokes.text: Elaine being chased through the streets by James's dogs, unable to get a cab
jokes.setup: Elaine left James's apartment and is now being pursued by the dogs that hated her
jokes.punchline: The image of Elaine running from dogs with no cab stopping — the entire city conspiring against her
jokes.explanation: The dog-chase payoff is inferred from dialogue but effective as physical comedy. The detail that no cabs stop is a nice New York-specific touch. Serves the plot but isn't the strongest comedic beat.

jokes.text: I'd ask you to stay tonight, but I only have the sofa bed, and it's where I sleep.
jokes.setup: Elaine has fled to James's apartment for safety
jokes.punchline: James's revelation that he sleeps on his own sofa bed — a detail that speaks volumes about his living situation and self-image
jokes.explanation: A quietly funny character detail — James's apartment is so small or his life so unsettled that he sleeps on the sofa bed. Sets up the head-to-toe sleeping arrangement with perfect economy.

jokes.text: We'll have to sleep head-to-toe. — Head-to-toe? — Head-to-toe.
jokes.setup: James offers Elaine the sofa bed, but they'll have to share it
jokes.punchline: The formality of 'head-to-toe' repeated three times — as if the arrangement is perfectly normal and not at all intimate
jokes.explanation: The triple repetition of 'head-to-toe' builds its own comedic rhythm. The phrase lands as both an awkward solution and a pseudo-chaste reassurance that satisfies no one. Sets up the later punchline about genitals.

jokes.text: James oversleeping because Elaine kicked him in the face all night — visual aftermath
jokes.setup: James was supposed to make wake-up calls at 6:30; he's a professional wake-up person
jokes.punchline: James himself is late and missed his clients because Elaine's feet were in his face all night
jokes.explanation: The irony of the wake-up service guy being unable to wake up because Elaine kicked him in the face is a beautifully constructed comedic payoff. Character integration is high — it's Elaine's chaotic energy disrupting everyone around her.

jokes.text: It's 8:30! — You were supposed to wake me up at 7:15. — I'm sorry. I didn't get any sleep. You kept kicking me in the face. — You're a wake-up guy. Don't you have calls to make? — I'll make 'em later.
jokes.setup: James is the professional wake-up caller; he failed to wake either himself or Elaine
jokes.punchline: 'I'll make 'em later.' — the wake-up guy's casual dismissal of his entire professional purpose
jokes.explanation: The cascade of irony — wake-up guy misses all calls, blames Elaine's feet, shrugs 'I'll make 'em later' — is very well constructed. The professionalism of his job vs. his complete dereliction of duty is a strong comedic contrast.

jokes.text: He's not here? — No, he's late again. — I got him a wake-up service. — Don't cover for him anymore. He'll be gone soon, and I'm gonna recommend you for his job.
jokes.setup: George got Morgan a wake-up service to help him — but the wake-up guy was incapacitated by Elaine
jokes.punchline: The chain of consequences: George's good deed backfired, Morgan is even later, and George is now being offered Morgan's job
jokes.explanation: The Seinfeld machinery at its best — good intentions producing disastrous outcomes. George tried to help Morgan, which led to Morgan being worse off, which leads to George being promoted. The interconnected consequences across storylines are the show's signature.

jokes.text: Sounds like all that winking got you a promotion.
jokes.setup: George is told he's being promoted because of his 'loyalty' — actually because of his involuntary winking
jokes.punchline: Jerry names the absurd causality: the grapefruit-induced wink led to a promotion
jokes.explanation: A perfect comedic summary of the episode's central absurd logic. Jerry's clean articulation of the cause-and-effect chain (winking → promotion) is the kind of meta-clarity Seinfeld does well. Great economy.

jokes.text: I don't want Morgan's job. He's got a lot of work to do.
jokes.setup: George is being offered a promotion to Morgan's position
jokes.punchline: George refuses — not on moral grounds, but because Morgan's job sounds like it involves actual work
jokes.explanation: Pure George Costanza character comedy. His rejection of a promotion because the job involves work is perfectly in-character — laziness elevated to a moral position. 'He's got a lot of work to do' is delivered with complete sincerity. High character integration.

jokes.text: Because they were in the pockets of my jacket. — They were? — Yes. — I was using them to spit out the mutton.
jokes.setup: Holly has been accusing Elaine of stealing her Grandma Mema napkins
jokes.punchline: The napkins were in Jerry's jacket — which he used to secretly spit out the mutton he was ostentatiously praising
jokes.explanation: A beautiful convergence of two separate plot threads — the napkins and the mutton. The reveal that Jerry was secretly disposing of the mutton he praised, using sacred Mema napkins, is a perfect Seinfeld payoff. Multiple callbacks land simultaneously.

jokes.text: I was almost mauled because of that mutton! — What exactly is mutton? — I don't know and I didn't want to find out.
jokes.setup: Elaine connects the dogs chasing her to the mutton smell on the jacket
jokes.punchline: Jerry's admission that he didn't know what mutton was and refused to find out
jokes.explanation: The mutton plotline achieves its full comedic payoff here: Jerry ate mystery meat, hid it in sacred napkins, left them in his jacket, which Elaine wore, attracting dogs who chased her. The causal chain is absurd but perfectly constructed. 'I didn't want to find out' is a great line.

jokes.text: Yeah, but we reversed positions, so there was no funny business.
jokes.setup: Jerry's eyebrows go up when he learns Elaine spent the night at James's
jokes.punchline: 'Reversed positions' — Elaine explains the head-to-toe arrangement as proof of innocence
jokes.explanation: Sets up the genitals punchline. The 'reversed positions' explanation is functional comedy — Elaine's earnest justification is inherently slightly absurd.

jokes.text: Reversed positions? Head-to-toe? So what? Your genitals are still lined up.
jokes.setup: Elaine claims head-to-toe sleeping proves no funny business occurred
jokes.punchline: Jerry's anatomical counter-argument: head-to-toe still aligns genitals
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. Jerry's logical refutation of head-to-toe as a chastity guarantee is a perfectly structured comedic argument — the geometric observation is correct, unexpected, and delivered with complete deadpan conviction. The phrase 'your genitals are still lined up' is quintessential Seinfeld — clinical and inappropriate simultaneously. High quotability.

jokes.text: No, because I slept with my back to him.
jokes.setup: Jerry argues that head-to-toe still aligns genitals
jokes.punchline: Elaine has a counter-counter-argument: she turned her back, which she delivers completely seriously
jokes.explanation: The button on the genitals exchange — Elaine's earnest anatomical rebuttal escalates the absurdity of the conversation. The comedy is in how seriously both parties are analyzing sofa-bed geometry.

jokes.text: Mr. O'Neill? — Yeah. — Look, you don't know me... — I can give you an autograph, but my pen's screwed up. You'll probably only get, like, half a P or something.
jokes.setup: Kramer approaches Paul O'Neill to ask a massive favor
jokes.punchline: O'Neill assumes Kramer wants an autograph and preemptively apologizes for his broken pen — the mundane misunderstanding before the actual absurd request
jokes.explanation: The misdirection of O'Neill thinking this is a routine autograph request — before Kramer reveals he wants custom home runs — is nicely structured. O'Neill's 'half a P' detail is a specific, funny bit of business. A good celebrity cameo joke.

jokes.text: You promised a kid in the hospital I'd hit two home runs? — Yeah. What, no good? — It's terrible. You don't hit home runs like that. It's hard. And where did you get two from? — Well, two is better than one.
jokes.setup: Kramer asks Paul O'Neill to hit two home runs for a sick kid
jokes.punchline: O'Neill's professional offense at the absurdity of the request; Kramer's defense: 'two is better than one'
jokes.explanation: O'Neill's genuine professional exasperation vs. Kramer's breezy 'two is better than one' is a great comedic pairing. The real baseball player reacting to cartoon logic is perfectly calibrated. 'What, no good?' is a very Kramer line.

jokes.text: Babe Ruth did it. — He did not. — You're saying Babe Ruth was a liar? — No, but he wasn't stupid enough to promise two.
jokes.setup: Kramer tries to justify promising two home runs by citing Babe Ruth
jokes.punchline: O'Neill's rebuttal: Ruth 'wasn't stupid enough to promise two' — a back-handed defense of the Babe
jokes.explanation: An excellent joke. Kramer invokes Babe Ruth's 'called shot' legend; O'Neill doesn't deny it happened but defends Ruth by saying he wasn't 'stupid enough to promise two.' The implicit acknowledgment that promising one is acceptable, two is insane, is a perfectly constructed comic distinction. High quotability.

jokes.text: Maybe I did overextend myself.
jokes.setup: O'Neill has just explained how impossible Kramer's two-home-run promise is
jokes.punchline: Kramer's mild, unbothered self-assessment: 'Maybe I did overextend myself'
jokes.explanation: A perfect Kramer button. His total lack of self-awareness combined with mild understatement ('overextend') is quintessential Kramer. The economy is superb — six words that are entirely character.

jokes.text: Bobby, can I have some of your juice? — After Paul O'Neill hits his first home run.
jokes.setup: Kramer is watching the Yankees game with Bobby in the hospital
jokes.punchline: Bobby is now using Paul O'Neill's future home runs as leverage — he's become a shrewd negotiator
jokes.explanation: Bobby withholding juice from an adult as leverage is darkly funny — the child has fully internalized the transactional dynamic Kramer established. The casual cruelty of a sick kid denying Kramer juice is a great comedic beat.

jokes.text: One more to go! [Kramer's jubilation as O'Neill hits the first home run]
jokes.setup: O'Neill has hit one home run; Kramer promised two
jokes.punchline: Kramer's celebration is immediately reoriented toward the second, impossible promise
jokes.explanation: Good escalation beat — the first home run is celebrated but immediately framed as half of an impossible promise. The comedy is in how Kramer has made a real baseball game into a personal transaction.

jokes.text: Bobby, it's very hard to hit two home runs in one game. Even for Paul O'Neill. — He can do it, Mr. Kramer. — I know he can. He'll do it for me.
jokes.setup: Kramer begins to have doubts as the game progresses without a second home run
jokes.punchline: Bobby now has more faith in the deal than Kramer does — 'He'll do it for me' is Bobby's earnest belief
jokes.explanation: The reversal of who believes in the promise is nicely constructed — the child's faith has outstripped the adult who made the promise. Adds a touch of pathos to the comedy.

jokes.text: O'Neill is safe at home. Inside-the-park home run! — He did it! He did it! — That's being scored a triple for Paul O'Neill, with a throwing error charged to Martinez.
jokes.setup: O'Neill rounds third and comes home — Kramer and Bobby celebrate the 'second home run'
jokes.punchline: The official scorer calls it a triple with an error — not a home run — instantly deflating the victory
jokes.explanation: A masterfully constructed comedic beat. The buildup through the inning — with Kramer and Bobby both fully invested — leads to a moment of apparent triumph, then is immediately negated by the official scorer. The specificity of 'throwing error charged to Martinez' makes it feel real. This is the episode's best structural joke — a genuine sports moment used to devastating comic effect.

jokes.text: That's not a home run. — Well, maybe not technically, but... — You said he could hit two home runs. Come on, give me that.
jokes.setup: Kramer tries to claim the inside-the-park triple/error as the 'second home run'
jokes.punchline: Bobby refuses — he knows the rules; Kramer's 'maybe not technically' is a pathetic retreat
jokes.explanation: Kramer trying to negotiate with a sick child over baseball scoring rules is peak Seinfeld absurdism. 'Maybe not technically' is a beautifully pathetic retreat from someone who promised two home runs with total confidence.

jokes.text: I usually like mine with an angioplasty.
jokes.setup: Holly asks if George likes his pork chop the way it's prepared
jokes.punchline: 'I usually like mine with an angioplasty' — implying his normal cooking produces heart-attack-level food
jokes.explanation: George's medical procedure as a seasoning choice is a great dark joke. The specificity of 'angioplasty' (rather than 'heart attack') elevates it — he's familiar enough with cardiac procedures to be precise. Very George.

jokes.text: What is everyone doing here? / What everyone does here: Cooking pork chops.
jokes.setup: Elaine arrives at Holly's to find a party she didn't know about.
jokes.punchline: Holly's deadpan 'What everyone does here: Cooking pork chops' — as if this is the self-evident universal activity.
jokes.explanation: Holly's confident answer about pork chop cooking as a universal activity is a good character beat. Slightly undercut by not being as sharp as the best Holly moments.

jokes.text: I bet he acted all aloof like he didn't know me. — A little. — That is so Franco.
jokes.setup: Holly says she greeted Jerry's butcher Franco on his behalf
jokes.punchline: 'That is so Franco' — delivered as if Franco's aloof behavior is a well-known personality trait, despite being a butcher Jerry has never deeply analyzed
jokes.explanation: 'That is so Franco' applies the grammar of celebrity gossip to a neighborhood butcher. Jerry's intimate knowledge of his butcher's personality quirks is a nice character touch. The phrase is memorable and lightly quotable.

jokes.text: We could argue all night over who took the napkins, but in today's modern world, it just doesn't seem relevant.
jokes.setup: Holly and Elaine are arguing about the missing Grandma Mema napkins
jokes.punchline: Jerry cuts the argument short with pompous pseudo-philosophical perspective: 'in today's modern world, it just doesn't seem relevant'
jokes.explanation: Jerry's breezy dismissal of a family argument using inflated philosophical language is a nice character moment. 'In today's modern world' is deliberately vapid — the 'nothing matters' worldview played as wisdom. Slightly meta in its acknowledgment that the napkin subplot is trivial.

jokes.text: George, by the way, tomorrow night, Paul O'Neill has to catch a fly ball in his hat.
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is happy with the birthday card; gives George praise
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner casually mentions that O'Neill must now catch a fly ball in his hat — a new impossible physical comedy demand delivered as a routine note
jokes.explanation: An outstanding escalation joke. Having just resolved the 'two home runs' subplot, Steinbrenner immediately assigns another impossible physical feat to O'Neill — 'catch a fly ball in his hat' — delivered with the same casual authority as a memo. The escalation of demands on Paul O'Neill is a perfect running joke payoff. George's silent reaction sells it.

jokes.text: George, this is beautiful! Why didn't you tell me you were gonna have it mounted like this? You were probably just gonna stick it in an envelope.
jokes.setup: Kramer has had the birthday card professionally mounted and framed
jokes.punchline: Steinbrenner credits George for the elaborate presentation; the irony is that George was literally going to 'stick it in an envelope' — Steinbrenner accidentally describes exactly what George planned
jokes.explanation: Steinbrenner accidentally describing George's original (lazy) plan as the thing he wisely avoided is a beautiful irony. The accidental accuracy of 'you were probably just gonna stick it in an envelope' is a great moment of dramatic irony — the audience knows he's right.

jokes.text: Well, you screwed me again, Costanza. How am I supposed to sign the card now? It's already under glass.
jokes.setup: Morgan was supposed to sign the birthday card; now it's framed under glass
jokes.punchline: George's attempt to protect his job by not giving Morgan the card has now permanently preserved the card without Morgan's signature
jokes.explanation: A satisfying closing irony — Morgan correctly identifies that George 'screwed him again,' but the specific mechanic (can't sign through glass) is a neat final twist. The accidental permanence of the framing is a good Seinfeld-style no-resolution resolution.

jokes.text: Hi, James. — Oh, this is, um, Holly... — Excuse me. What are those dogs wearing? — Bandannas. — Aren't they cute?
jokes.setup: James arrives with his dogs; Holly is there; tense reunion
jokes.punchline: The dogs are wearing Grandma Mema's napkins as bandannas — the napkins' final fate
jokes.explanation: The supreme convergence of two running plot threads — the Mema napkins and James's dogs. The sacred napkins ended up as dog bandannas after passing through Jerry's jacket pockets as mutton-spitting cloths. This is the kind of multi-thread payoff Seinfeld is famous for. The visual of dogs wearing Mema's prized napkins as bandannas is an excellent visual gag that rewards the episode's entire narrative architecture.

jokes.text: The dogs did that [to the jacket], but it wasn't their fault. Somebody stuffed some strange meat in the pockets. — Was it mutton? — Could have been. — You always stuff meat in your pockets? — Uh... sometimes I use the sofa.
jokes.setup: Jerry's jacket has been destroyed by the dogs
jokes.punchline: Jerry's confession that he uses the sofa as an alternative meat-disposal location — revealing a practiced routine of hiding unwanted food
jokes.explanation: A great final reveal — Jerry's pocket-stuffing wasn't a one-time thing. 'Sometimes I use the sofa' implies an established system of food disposal that is both disgusting and completely Jerry-plausible. This is excellent character comedy with high quotability.

jokes.text: You always stuff meat in your pockets? / Uh... sometimes I use the sofa.
jokes.setup: Holly confronts Jerry about hiding meat in his pockets.
jokes.punchline: Jerry's admission — 'sometimes I use the sofa' — as a routine meat-disposal method, delivered as if this is normal.
jokes.explanation: Possibly the best single comedy line of the episode. 'Sometimes I use the sofa' implies an established, ongoing practice of hiding uneaten food in furniture — as if this is a normalized adult behavior. The non-apology, the normalcy of it, and the image it creates are all perfect. High economy, originality, and the character integration is excellent because only Jerry could deliver this with sufficient deadpan.

jokes.text: George is summoned to Steinbrenner's office and given Morgan's job; Steinbrenner's list of fired managers: Yogi Berra, Lou Piniella, Bucky Dent, Billy Martin [x4], Dallas Green, Dick Hauser, Bill Virdon, Stump Merrill, Bob Lemon, Gene Michael, Buck Showalter...
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner is telling George about the painful necessity of letting people go, claiming 'I've had to let a few people go over the years'
jokes.punchline: The list of fired managers is enormous — and Billy Martin appears four separate times, with increasing comic effect each time
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic peak alongside the hospital scene. 'A few people' leading into a recitation of virtually every Yankees manager in Steinbrenner history — with Billy Martin appearing four times — is a perfect escalation joke rooted in real New York Yankees lore. The recurring 'Billy Martin' gets funnier each time, building to a near-absurdist rhythm. This is exceptional Seinfeld: grounded in real detail, escalated to absurdity, character-perfect. The craft is high because it requires knowledge of Yankees history to fully land and because the repetition is perfectly calibrated — four Billy Martins is exactly right.

jokes.text: ...Gene Michael, Buck Showalter... — Uh, George! — You didn't hear that from me. — George?
jokes.setup: Steinbrenner has been listing all the managers he's fired; George has quietly passed out or zoned out
jokes.punchline: George doesn't respond — he's been overwhelmed (or has fainted). Steinbrenner's 'You didn't hear that from me' implies he's said too much, then his confused 'George?' when there's no response.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the Steinbrenner monologue. Steinbrenner catching himself ('You didn't hear that from me') then realizing George is not responsive is a great closing beat. The implication that the list alone was enough to incapacitate George is funny. 'George?' lands as the episode's final image.

tmdb_overview: George's involuntary winking is misinterpreted by many, and prompts Kramer to seek a favor from Yankee Paul O'Neill; Jerry tries to hide his distaste for meat from Elaine's cousin.
insight: Character comedy drives 78-point episode as Kramer's wink bit escalates across four separate scenes.
